Now lets code: create a file, inside this new pkg, called nav_server.py and paste this code:

In [ ]: Copied! 
#!/usr/bin/env python3

import rclpy
from rclpy.node import Node
from rclpy.action import ActionServer
from rclpy.action.server import CancelResponse  
from rclpy.executors import MultiThreadedExecutor
from rclpy.callback_groups import MutuallyExclusiveCallbackGroup

from geometry_msgs.msg import Twist
from nav_msgs.msg import Odometry
import math
import time

from amrj16_messages.action import DriveDistance

def get_yaw_from_quaternion(q):
    siny_cosp = 2 * (q.w * q.z + q.x * q.y)
    cosy_cosp = 1 - 2 * (q.y * q.y + q.z * q.z)
    return math.atan2(siny_cosp, cosy_cosp)

class DriveActionServer(Node):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__('drive_action_server')
        
        self.action_group = MutuallyExclusiveCallbackGroup()
        self.odom_group = MutuallyExclusiveCallbackGroup()

        self.cmd_vel_pub = self.create_publisher(Twist, '/cmd_vel', 10)
        
        self.current_x = 0.0
        self.current_y = 0.0
        self.current_yaw = 0.0
        self.odom_received = False
        
        # variable to keep track of the currently running goal in case of different goal sent together
        self.active_goal_handle = None
        
        self.odom_sub = self.create_subscription(
            Odometry, '/odom', self.odom_callback, 10, callback_group=self.odom_group)

        self._action_server = ActionServer(
            self, DriveDistance, 'drive_distance', 
            execute_callback=self.execute_callback, 
            cancel_callback=self.cancel_callback, 
            callback_group=self.action_group)
            
        self.get_logger().info('Nav Action Server is READY.')

    def odom_callback(self, msg):
        self.current_x = msg.pose.pose.position.x
        self.current_y = msg.pose.pose.position.y
        self.current_yaw = get_yaw_from_quaternion(msg.pose.pose.orientation)
        self.odom_received = True

    #callback allows the server to accept Ctrl+C cancellations
    def cancel_callback(self, goal_handle):
        self.get_logger().info('Received cancel request (Ctrl+C)!')
        return CancelResponse.ACCEPT

    def execute_callback(self, goal_handle):
        #  if there is already a goal running, we must abort it before starting this new one
        if self.active_goal_handle is not None and self.active_goal_handle.is_active:
            self.get_logger().warn('A new goal arrived! Aborting the previous goal...')
            self.active_goal_handle.abort()
            time.sleep(0.2)  

        #set this new goal as the active one
        self.active_goal_handle = goal_handle

        target_x = goal_handle.request.target_x
        target_y = goal_handle.request.target_y
        self.get_logger().info(f'Executing goal: x: {target_x}, y: {target_y}')
        
        while not self.odom_received:
            self.get_logger().warn('Waiting for odom data...', throttle_duration_sec=2.0)
            time.sleep(0.1)

        feedback_msg = DriveDistance.Feedback()
        cmd_msg = Twist()

        # ==========================================
        # PHASE 1: TURN TO FACE TARGET
        # ==========================================
        target_angle = math.atan2(target_y - self.current_y, target_x - self.current_x)
        angle_diff = target_angle - self.current_yaw
        angle_diff = math.atan2(math.sin(angle_diff), math.cos(angle_diff))
        
        self.get_logger().info('Phase 1: Turning towards target...')
        while abs(angle_diff) > 0.05:
            
            if goal_handle.is_cancel_requested:
                return self.cancel_goal(goal_handle)
                
            
            if not goal_handle.is_active:
                self.stop_robot()
                result = DriveDistance.Result()
                result.success = False
                result.message = "Aborted by a newer goal."
                return result

            target_angle = math.atan2(target_y - self.current_y, target_x - self.current_x)
            angle_diff = target_angle - self.current_yaw
            angle_diff = math.atan2(math.sin(angle_diff), math.cos(angle_diff))

            cmd_msg.linear.x = 0.0
            if (angle_diff > 0):
                cmd_msg.angular.z = 0.3 
            else:
                cmd_msg.angular.z = -0.3

            self.cmd_vel_pub.publish(cmd_msg)
            time.sleep(0.05)

        self.stop_robot()
        self.get_logger().info('Finished Turning.')

        # ==========================================
        # PHASE 2: DRIVE TO TARGET
        # ==========================================
        self.get_logger().info('Phase 2: Approaching target...')
        
        def get_distance():
            return math.sqrt((target_x - self.current_x)**2 + (target_y - self.current_y)**2)
            
        distance_remaining = get_distance()

        while distance_remaining > 0.1:
            
            if goal_handle.is_cancel_requested:
                return self.cancel_goal(goal_handle)

            
            if not goal_handle.is_active:
                self.stop_robot()
                result = DriveDistance.Result()
                result.success = False
                result.message = "Aborted by a newer goal."
                return result

            distance_remaining = get_distance()

            cmd_msg.linear.x = 0.2
            cmd_msg.angular.z = 0.0
            self.cmd_vel_pub.publish(cmd_msg)
            
            feedback_msg.distance_remaining = distance_remaining
            goal_handle.publish_feedback(feedback_msg)
            
            time.sleep(0.1)

        # ==========================================
        # GOAL REACHED
        # ==========================================
        self.stop_robot()
        goal_handle.succeed()
        
        result = DriveDistance.Result()
        result.success = True
        result.message = "Successfully arrived at destination."
        self.get_logger().info('Goal succeeded.')
        
        return result

    def cancel_goal(self, goal_handle):
        goal_handle.canceled()
        self.stop_robot()
        self.get_logger().warn('Goal canceled by Client (Ctrl+C).')
        result = DriveDistance.Result()
        result.success = False
        result.message = "Goal Canceled"
        return result

    def stop_robot(self):
        stop_msg = Twist()
        stop_msg.linear.x = 0.0
        stop_msg.angular.z = 0.0
        self.cmd_vel_pub.publish(stop_msg)

def main(args=None):
    rclpy.init(args=args)
    node = DriveActionServer()
    executor = MultiThreadedExecutor(num_threads=3)
    try:
        rclpy.spin(node, executor=executor)
    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        pass
    finally:
        node.destroy_node()
        rclpy.shutdown()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()

Now create the action client file called drive_client.py and paste this code:

In [ ]: Copied! 
#!/usr/bin/env python3

import rclpy
from rclpy.node import Node
from rclpy.action import ActionClient

from amrj16_messages.action import DriveDistance

class DriveActionClient(Node):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__('drive_action_client')
        self._action_client = ActionClient(self, DriveDistance, 'drive_distance')

    def send_goal(self,goal_target_x, goal_target_y):
        self.get_logger().info('Waiting for Action Server...')
        self._action_client.wait_for_server()

        goal_msg = DriveDistance.Goal()
        goal_msg.target_x = goal_target_x
        goal_msg.target_y = goal_target_y

        self.get_logger().info(f'Sending Goal: x: {goal_target_x}, y: {goal_target_y}')
        
        # send goal async, and attach the feedback callback
        self._send_goal_future = self._action_client.send_goal_async(
            goal_msg, 
            feedback_callback=self.feedback_callback)
            
        self._send_goal_future.add_done_callback(self.goal_response_callback)

    def feedback_callback(self, feedback_msg):
        # this function executes every time the server sends a progress update
        feedback = feedback_msg.feedback
        self.get_logger().info(f'Distance: {feedback.distance_remaining}')

    def goal_response_callback(self, future):
        goal_handle = future.result()
        if not goal_handle.accepted:
            self.get_logger().error('Goal rejected by server.')
            return

        self.get_logger().info('Goal accepted! Waiting for final result...')
        self._get_result_future = goal_handle.get_result_async()
        self._get_result_future.add_done_callback(self.get_result_callback)

    def get_result_callback(self, future):
        result = future.result().result
        self.get_logger().info(f'Drive result: {result.message}')
        
        # shut down the client once the goal is reached
        rclpy.shutdown()

def main(args=None):
    rclpy.init(args=args)
    action_client = DriveActionClient()
    
    goal_target_x = 0.0
    goal_target_y = 1.0
    action_client.send_goal(goal_target_x, goal_target_y)
    
    rclpy.spin(action_client)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
#!/usr/bin/env python3 import rclpy from rclpy.node import Node from rclpy.action import ActionClient from amrj16_messages.action import DriveDistance class DriveActionClient(Node): def __init__(self): super().__init__('drive_action_client') self._action_client = ActionClient(self, DriveDistance, 'drive_distance') def send_goal(self,goal_target_x, goal_target_y): self.get_logger().info('Waiting for Action Server...') self._action_client.wait_for_server() goal_msg = DriveDistance.Goal() goal_msg.target_x = goal_target_x goal_msg.target_y = goal_target_y self.get_logger().info(f'Sending Goal: x: {goal_target_x}, y: {goal_target_y}') # send goal async, and attach the feedback callback self._send_goal_future = self._action_client.send_goal_async( goal_msg, feedback_callback=self.feedback_callback) self._send_goal_future.add_done_callback(self.goal_response_callback) def feedback_callback(self, feedback_msg): # this function executes every time the server sends a progress update feedback = feedback_msg.feedback self.get_logger().info(f'Distance: {feedback.distance_remaining}') def goal_response_callback(self, future): goal_handle = future.result() if not goal_handle.accepted: self.get_logger().error('Goal rejected by server.') return self.get_logger().info('Goal accepted! Waiting for final result...') self._get_result_future = goal_handle.get_result_async() self._get_result_future.add_done_callback(self.get_result_callback) def get_result_callback(self, future): result = future.result().result self.get_logger().info(f'Drive result: {result.message}') # shut down the client once the goal is reached rclpy.shutdown() def main(args=None): rclpy.init(args=args) action_client = DriveActionClient() goal_target_x = 0.0 goal_target_y = 1.0 action_client.send_goal(goal_target_x, goal_target_y) rclpy.spin(action_client) if __name__ == '__main__': main()

#!/usr/bin/env python3

import rclpy
from rclpy.node import Node
from rclpy.action import ActionClient

# Standard messages
from std_msgs.msg import Float32, String

# Custom interfaces (Ensure these match your actual package name)
from amrj16_messages.srv import GetStation
from amrj16_messages.action import DriveDistance

class RobotDockingMaster(Node):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__('robot_docking_master')
        
        # SUBSCRIBER: Battery Monitor
        self.battery_sub = self.create_subscription(
            Float32, '/battery_status', self.battery_callback, 10)
            
        # PUBLISHER: Safety Alert
        self.alert_pub = self.create_publisher(
            String, '/safety_alert', 10)
            
        # SERVICE CLIENT: To get coordinates
        self.station_client = self.create_client(
            GetStation, 'get_charging_station')
            
        # ACTION CLIENT: To drive the robot
        self.drive_action_client = ActionClient(
            self, DriveDistance, 'drive_distance')
        
        # State flag: ensures we only initiate the sequence ONCE
        self.charging_sequence_active = False
        
        self.get_logger().info('Robot Docking Master is running and monitoring battery...')

    # ==========================================
    # BATTERY MONITORING
    # ==========================================
    def battery_callback(self, msg):
        # Print battery status periodically (Optional, you can comment this out to reduce spam)
        # self.get_logger().info(f'Battery Level: {msg.data}%')

        #trigger sequence if battery <75% AND we aren't already handling it
        if msg.data < 75.0 and not self.charging_sequence_active:
            self.charging_sequence_active = True
            
            self.get_logger().warn(f'BATTERY LOW ({msg.data}%). Initiating Return to Base!')
            
            #publish alert message
            alert_msg = String()
            alert_msg.data = f"[WARNING] Battery Low: {msg.data}%. Heading to charging station."
            self.alert_pub.publish(alert_msg)
            
            # request coordinates first
            self.request_station_coordinates("station_1")

    # ==========================================
    # SERVICE CLIENT LOGIC (Get Coordinates)
    # ==========================================
    def request_station_coordinates(self, station_name):
        self.get_logger().info(f'Requesting coordinates for {station_name}...')
        
        if not self.station_client.wait_for_service(timeout_sec=3.0):
            self.get_logger().error('Station Service is not available! Aborting sequence.')
            self.charging_sequence_active = False # Reset so it can try again
            return
            
        req = GetStation.Request()
        req.station_name = station_name
        
        # Async call to prevent freezing the ROS 2 node
        future = self.station_client.call_async(req)
        future.add_done_callback(self.station_response_callback)

    def station_response_callback(self, future):
        try:
            response = future.result()
            if response.success:
                # if success we have the coordiantes of the station 
                self.get_logger().info(f'Received Coordinates -> X: {response.x}, Y: {response.y}')
                
                self.send_drive_goal(response.x, response.y)
            else:
                self.get_logger().error('Failed to get station coordinates (Station not found).')
                self.charging_sequence_active = False
        except Exception as e:
            self.get_logger().error(f'Service call failed: {e}')
            self.charging_sequence_active = False

    # ==========================================
    # ACTION CLIENT LOGIC (Drive to Station)
    # ==========================================
    def send_drive_goal(self, target_x, target_y):
        self.get_logger().info('Waiting for Drive Action Server...')
        
        if not self.drive_action_client.wait_for_server(timeout_sec=3.0):
            self.get_logger().error('Drive Action Server not available! Aborting sequence.')
            self.charging_sequence_active = False
            return

        goal_msg = DriveDistance.Goal()
        goal_msg.target_x = target_x
        goal_msg.target_y = target_y

        self.get_logger().info(f'Sending robot to X: {target_x}, Y: {target_y}')
        
        # Send goal async, attach feedback callback
        self._send_goal_future = self.drive_action_client.send_goal_async(
            goal_msg, feedback_callback=self.drive_feedback_callback)
            
        self._send_goal_future.add_done_callback(self.drive_goal_response_callback)

    def drive_feedback_callback(self, feedback_msg):
        # prints the live distance remaining sent by the Action Server nice for see how much distance is missing
        distance = feedback_msg.feedback.distance_remaining
        self.get_logger().info(f'Approaching station... Distance remaining: {distance:.2f} m')

    def drive_goal_response_callback(self, future):
        goal_handle = future.result()
        if not goal_handle.accepted:
            self.get_logger().error('Drive goal rejected by server!')
            self.charging_sequence_active = False
            return

        self.get_logger().info('Drive goal accepted. Moving...')
        
        # Wait for the final result
        self._get_result_future = goal_handle.get_result_async()
        self._get_result_future.add_done_callback(self.drive_result_callback)

    def drive_result_callback(self, future):
        result = future.result().result
        
        if result.success:
            self.get_logger().info(f'SUCCESS: {result.message}')
            self.get_logger().info('Robot has successfully docked and is now charging.')
            
            # here probably the robot will spend time to recharge so we don't set the self.charging_sequence_active = False in order to not 
            # allow the robot to start the sequence again, there is not charging.
        else:
            self.get_logger().error(f'FAILED: {result.message}')
            self.charging_sequence_active = False # Allow retrying

def main(args=None):
    rclpy.init(args=args)
    node = RobotDockingMaster()
    
    try:
        rclpy.spin(node)
    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        pass
    finally:
        node.destroy_node()
        rclpy.shutdown()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
#!/usr/bin/env python3 import rclpy from rclpy.node import Node from rclpy.action import ActionClient # Standard messages from std_msgs.msg import Float32, String # Custom interfaces (Ensure these match your actual package name) from amrj16_messages.srv import GetStation from amrj16_messages.action import DriveDistance class RobotDockingMaster(Node): def __init__(self): super().__init__('robot_docking_master') # SUBSCRIBER: Battery Monitor self.battery_sub = self.create_subscription( Float32, '/battery_status', self.battery_callback, 10) # PUBLISHER: Safety Alert self.alert_pub = self.create_publisher( String, '/safety_alert', 10) # SERVICE CLIENT: To get coordinates self.station_client = self.create_client( GetStation, 'get_charging_station') # ACTION CLIENT: To drive the robot self.drive_action_client = ActionClient( self, DriveDistance, 'drive_distance') # State flag: ensures we only initiate the sequence ONCE self.charging_sequence_active = False self.get_logger().info('Robot Docking Master is running and monitoring battery...') # ========================================== # BATTERY MONITORING # ========================================== def battery_callback(self, msg): # Print battery status periodically (Optional, you can comment this out to reduce spam) # self.get_logger().info(f'Battery Level: {msg.data}%') #trigger sequence if battery <75% AND we aren't already handling it if msg.data < 75.0 and not self.charging_sequence_active: self.charging_sequence_active = True self.get_logger().warn(f'BATTERY LOW ({msg.data}%). Initiating Return to Base!') #publish alert message alert_msg = String() alert_msg.data = f"[WARNING] Battery Low: {msg.data}%. Heading to charging station." self.alert_pub.publish(alert_msg) # request coordinates first self.request_station_coordinates("station_1") # ========================================== # SERVICE CLIENT LOGIC (Get Coordinates) # ========================================== def request_station_coordinates(self, station_name): self.get_logger().info(f'Requesting coordinates for {station_name}...') if not self.station_client.wait_for_service(timeout_sec=3.0): self.get_logger().error('Station Service is not available! Aborting sequence.') self.charging_sequence_active = False # Reset so it can try again return req = GetStation.Request() req.station_name = station_name # Async call to prevent freezing the ROS 2 node future = self.station_client.call_async(req) future.add_done_callback(self.station_response_callback) def station_response_callback(self, future): try: response = future.result() if response.success: # if success we have the coordiantes of the station self.get_logger().info(f'Received Coordinates -> X: {response.x}, Y: {response.y}') self.send_drive_goal(response.x, response.y) else: self.get_logger().error('Failed to get station coordinates (Station not found).') self.charging_sequence_active = False except Exception as e: self.get_logger().error(f'Service call failed: {e}') self.charging_sequence_active = False # ========================================== # ACTION CLIENT LOGIC (Drive to Station) # ========================================== def send_drive_goal(self, target_x, target_y): self.get_logger().info('Waiting for Drive Action Server...') if not self.drive_action_client.wait_for_server(timeout_sec=3.0): self.get_logger().error('Drive Action Server not available! Aborting sequence.') self.charging_sequence_active = False return goal_msg = DriveDistance.Goal() goal_msg.target_x = target_x goal_msg.target_y = target_y self.get_logger().info(f'Sending robot to X: {target_x}, Y: {target_y}') # Send goal async, attach feedback callback self._send_goal_future = self.drive_action_client.send_goal_async( goal_msg, feedback_callback=self.drive_feedback_callback) self._send_goal_future.add_done_callback(self.drive_goal_response_callback) def drive_feedback_callback(self, feedback_msg): # prints the live distance remaining sent by the Action Server nice for see how much distance is missing distance = feedback_msg.feedback.distance_remaining self.get_logger().info(f'Approaching station... Distance remaining: {distance:.2f} m') def drive_goal_response_callback(self, future): goal_handle = future.result() if not goal_handle.accepted: self.get_logger().error('Drive goal rejected by server!') self.charging_sequence_active = False return self.get_logger().info('Drive goal accepted. Moving...') # Wait for the final result self._get_result_future = goal_handle.get_result_async() self._get_result_future.add_done_callback(self.drive_result_callback) def drive_result_callback(self, future): result = future.result().result if result.success: self.get_logger().info(f'SUCCESS: {result.message}') self.get_logger().info('Robot has successfully docked and is now charging.') # here probably the robot will spend time to recharge so we don't set the self.charging_sequence_active = False in order to not # allow the robot to start the sequence again, there is not charging. else: self.get_logger().error(f'FAILED: {result.message}') self.charging_sequence_active = False # Allow retrying def main(args=None): rclpy.init(args=args) node = RobotDockingMaster() try: rclpy.spin(node) except KeyboardInterrupt: pass finally: node.destroy_node() rclpy.shutdown() if __name__ == '__main__': main() In [ ]: Copied!

docs.title: ROS 2 Basic Tutorial: Autonomous Docking with the J16MOTO## Chapter 1: Battery Management with ROS 2 Humble¶
docs.text:
In this tutorial, we aim to demonstrate how to develop from scratch a testing functionality for a battery management used in the navigation of an indoor autonomous robot operating in an industrial-like environment, the J16 MOTO robot. The objective is to provide a practical guide to developing a simple application in ROS 2, illustrating the main communication mechanisms and node management concepts required to interface with the robot’s sensors and verify their correct operation within a real or simulated robotic system.

To achieve this, several fundamental ROS 2 concepts will be introduced and applied to the amrj16_ultimac platform, which provide the foundation for developing modular and scalable robotic applications.

In particular, the tutorial will cover the following topics:

  1. Basic ROS 2 concepts: packages, launch files, nodes, and clients.

  2. Topic Pub/Sub: the communication mechanism between nodes based on message publishing and subscription

  3. Custom interfaces: how to define custom message types to structure and exchange data between nodes.

  4. Service Client/Server: how to request the robot to perform an operation and receive a synchronous or asynchronous response.

  5. Action Client/Server: how to manage long-running tasks while receiving progress updates.

  6. Multithreading: techniques that allow multiple operations to be executed concurrently within a node.

  7. Debug tools: the use of RViz and logging systems to monitor and analyze the robot’s behavior.

By the end of this tutorial, the reader will have acquired the fundamental concepts needed to develop and test several functionalities based on ROS 2, applicable to the development of the J16 MOTO robot in industrial environments.

docs.title: Step 0: Setting up the Workshop¶
docs.text: This is a very important step in order to proceed with the tutorial, this because the environment and the OS version must be fully compatible. In order to start correctly we need to have ROS 2 Humble and ubuntu 22.04, to check if we accomplish this requirements we need to open a terminal and write this command:

docs.title: Step 1: Clone the repository¶
docs.text: The first step is to clone the repository where all the code you need for the future tutorial are. don't worry we will write some new code in order to learn better.

docs.title: Step 2: Complie the workspace¶
docs.text: Let's compile our newly created workspace to ensure everything is perfectly set up. The command to use are:

docs.title: introduction¶
docs.text:
Let’s move to a more practical perspective. Since we want to develop an application in this case, a battery management tool it is necessary to encapsulate it within what is called a package in ROS 2.

The package defines the environment where the application is organized and developed. Inside the package we implement the functionalities of the application. These functionalities are executed by nodes, which represent the running processes of the system.

Finally, the nodes are started and managed through launch files, which allow the different components of the application to be executed together.

docs.title: Creating ROS 2 Package¶
docs.text: A package is the fundamental unit of ROS 2. You cannot run a ROS 2 node, launch file, or configuration if it is not inside a package. It's like a container for your code.

docs.title: Python vs C++:¶
docs.text:
In ROS 2, you can write packages in Python (ament_python) or C++ (ament_cmake). C++ is faster and usfull for some things, but Python is much easier to read and prototype. Since this tutorial is "Fundamentals of ROS 2" in Python, we will go with this.

Let's create our first package right inside your ros2_ws2/src folder.

docs.title: Creating a ROS 2 Node¶
docs.text:
Now it is time to create our first ROS 2 node, but what is a node?

A node is an entity that contains and executes a program responsible for performing a specific task within the robotic system. For example, in our application we want to monitor the state of the robot battery. The program that reads and analyzes the battery information will run inside a node.

Each node is designed to handle a specific function of the robot. In this case, the node will be responsible for acquiring, processing, and monitoring the battery status, allowing the system to track important parameters such as charge level and overall battery condition.

Open VSCode and go inside our package, than inside the folder called amrj16_battery_management and create a new file called battery_status_node.

At the end you will have something like this:

docs.title: Creating a ROS 2 Launch File¶
docs.text:
Right now, starting our node with ros2 run is easy. But imagine you are working on a real robot. You will have a node for the camera, a node for the laser scanner, a node for the motors...

To resolve this we use Launch Files. A launch file is a Python script whose only job is to start other nodes. Let's create one for our amrj16_battery_management package. First, we need to create a launch folder inside our package, and then write the python launch script. follow this command:

docs.title: Introduction¶
docs.text:
In ROS 2, nodes need a way to exchange data. The most common and fundamental communication method is the Topic. Topics are primarily used for continuous data streams, such as sensor readings (e.g., battery managment system (BMS), laser scans, camera frames) or continuous control commands (e.g., velocity commands to motors).

What is a ROS Topic?

  1. A ROS Topic implements a Publisher/Subscriber architecture.
  2. Publisher: A node that sends data to a specific topic name.
  3. Subscriber: A node that listens to a specific topic name to receive data.
  4. Decoupling: Publishers and subscribers do not know about each other's existence. A node simply broadcasts data to a topic, and any number of nodes can subscribe to that topic to read the data. This allows for a highly modular and decoupled system (one-to-many, many-to-one, or many-to-many communication).

docs.title: Battery Status Topic¶
docs.text:
Now we will modify our node to add this functionality to our battery_status node. This will add a topic where we will write the robot's battery level. So we will publish this information to the topic: /battery_status.

Our code now let us know that the battery is handled and now it subscribes to the battery topic the battery information. Copy and paste this into battery_status.py.

docs.title: How to mix publisher and subscriber.¶
docs.text:
Now we just explore the basic fundation of how the information flow in ROS 2 but in real robotic application the recived information are always used to publish other information, and a single node usually has a mixture of subscriber and publisher. Now we will create a basic node containig both.

Precisely, our code will read the information from the battery_status topic and publish an alert if the battery level falls below a safety threshold on a new topic called /safety_alert.

Now create a new file called battery_monitor.py in the amrj16_battery_management folder and paste this code:

docs.title: Create a custom Interface for topics¶
docs.text:
Sometimes the standard available messages in ROS 2 is not enough detailed for a custom pourpose, to overcome this ROS 2 allow to create a custom interface in order to create exactly what someone needs.

For this example we will create an interface that gives us the information about the battery but also its temperature which in some cases could affect the battery duration, and instead of publish this information into two topic we will have this interface called /battery_status which contains both the parameters.

In ROS 2, custom interfaces (.msg, .srv, .action) should ideally be placed in a dedicated ament_cmake package. Even if your logic nodes are written in Python, the message generation system relies on CMake.

Fortunally, the repository we created previously has already a folder for storing this custom interfaces and its called amrj16_messages

docs.title: Introduction¶
docs.text: While Topics (Publisher/Subscriber) are designed for continuous streams of data, there are many situations in robotics where you need to trigger a specific action and wait for a result. For example: "Turn on the diagnostic LED", "Compute a path to coordinates X,Y", or "Save the current map." For these discrete, request-and-response interactions, we use Services.

docs.title: What is a service¶
docs.text:
A ROS 2 Service implements a Client/Server architecture.

  1. Service Server: A node that offers a specific capability. It waits idly. When it receives a Request, it processes it, executes the associated function, and sends back a Response.
  2. Service Client: A node that sends a Request to the server and waits to receive the Response.
  3. Unlike Topics (which are 1-to-many or many-to-many), Services are strictly 1-to-1 during a specific call. Multiple clients can request the same service, but the server handles them individually.

To better understand this we will create a service that activates a "Charge Mode" on the robot. We will use a standard ROS 2 service type: std_srvs/srv/SetBool.

A .srv file is split into two parts by three dashes (---). The top is the Request, the bottom is the Response. For SetBool, it looks like this:

docs.title: Synchronous vs Asynchronous Service Client¶
docs.text:
When writing a Service Client in ROS 2 Python, you have two choices:

  1. Synchronous (call()): The node stops everything it is doing and waits for the server to reply. Which in ROS 2, making a synchronous call inside a callback will cause a deadlock and freeze your entire node. This is not always the right pattern.
  2. Asynchronous (call_async()): The node sends the request and receives a "Future" (a placeholder for the response). The node continues executing (spinning) and processes the response only when it arrives. This is way more reliable.

docs.title: Custom service¶
docs.text:
Just like topic we can create our custom service in order to have the maximum flexibility of our system. To do so we just need to create the pkg for the custom messages, this time with ament_cmake, just like for custom topics, the pkg is the same. Inside this pkg we need to create the directoy called srv where we will put our custom services. For this section we will prepare the ground for the final application described at the start of the tutorial.

So we will use the service paradighm for develop a service which, after a client call, will gives us back the position of the docking station of the robot, needed for the recharge. this position will be read inside a config file which can be customized.

The first things to do is to create the config file, in ROS the good practice is to put all the config file inside a folder in the workspace called config, in this case amrj16_config.

So step one: create the config folder called amrj16_config and the stations.yaml file:

inside this file we will write the coordinates of the stations, for now just add one station very simple, at the spawn of the robot, so when the robot is discharging it will comeback to the starting position.

docs.title: Introduction¶
docs.text:
In standard software development, code usually executes sequentially from top to bottom. However, robotics systems operate in an asynchronous and unpredictable environment. You do not know exactly when a sensor will send data, when an emergency stop button will be pressed, or when a remote server will reply to a request. To handle this unpredictability without freezing the entire program, ROS 2 relies heavily on an event-driven architecture based on Callbacks.

The standard function executes synchronously. You define the function, and you explicitly call it in your code. The program waits for the function to finish before moving to the next line.

A callback function is a function that you define, but you never explicitly call it in your code. Instead, you pass the function's name (its reference) to the ROS 2 system. You are effectively telling ROS 2: "Keep this function ready. Whenever a specific event occurs, execute this code."

There are few possible callback triggers such as:

  1. A message arriving on a Topic (Subscriber callback).
  2. A timer reaching its designated period (Timer callback).
  3. A Service request arriving from a client (Service callback).

Until now we have alredy used this callback function in most of our code, for example in the battery_monitor.py with the battery_callback but also in the status client and server. We have also use a function, at the end of our codes, called rclpy.spin(node). This is a blocking function that takes complet control of the thread and runs an infinite loop of the file, this is necessary for example for the data incoming for a laser scan, ideally the code that must handle this data must run continuously in order to detect obstacles. Also our battery monitor must run with this infinite loops in order to detect when the battery drops.

Of course robotics has many different applicationa nd also the possibility of just run once our callback could be usefull for checks the ROS 2 network for any pending events, and then immediately returns control to your script. This allows you to mix ROS 2 communication with your own custom infinite loops. This possibility is rclpy.spin_once(node, timeout_sec).

docs.title: Introduction¶
docs.text: By default, ROS 2 nodes execute in a Single-Threaded Executor. This means that all callbacks (timers, subscribers, services) are placed into a single queue and executed one by one. If one callback is running, all other callbacks are blocked and must wait their turn. While this makes data management easy (you don't have to worry about race conditions), it is a severe limitation for real-world robotics.

docs.title: Why Multithreading?¶
docs.text: Imagine an autonomous mobile robot (AMR). It has a laser scanner callback that processes navigation data, taking 100 milliseconds to compute. If the navigation callback starts running right before probably it will hits an obstacle. Multithreading solves this by assigning different callbacks to different CPU threads, allowing the fast, critical callbacks to execute simultaneously alongside the slow, heavy computation callbacks. To enable this, ROS 2 uses the MultiThreadedExecutor.

docs.title: Why Callback Groups?¶
docs.text: If we simply enable the MultiThreadedExecutor, ROS 2 will grab any available thread to execute any triggered callback. This introduces a dangerous problem: Race Conditions. If the same callback is triggered twice very quickly, the executor might run it simultaneously on two different threads. If that callback modifies a critical variable (like motor speed), both threads will try to write to that variable at the exact same time, causing erratic behavior or crashes. To control this chaos, ROS 2 provides Callback Groups. They act as rules telling the executor how it is allowed to assign threads to callbacks.

docs.title: Reentrant Callback Group¶
docs.text: Callbacks assigned to a ReentrantCallbackGroup are allowed to execute in parallel without restrictions. Different callbacks in this group can run at the same time. The same callback can even overlap with itself if triggered multiple times. Use case: Stateless functions that only read data and do not modify shared variables (e.g., coordinate frame transformations).

docs.title: Mutually Exclusive Callback Group¶
docs.text: Callbacks assigned to a MutuallyExclusiveCallbackGroup are protected. If a callback in this group is running, no other callback in the same group is allowed to run. However, callbacks in different Mutually Exclusive Callback Groups can run in parallel. Use case: Protecting hardware resources (like a serial port) or shared variables. If you put your laser scanner callback in Group A and your safety bumper in Group B, they can run in parallel safely.

docs.title: Introduction¶
docs.text: In industrial robotics, some commands take a long time to complete. Topics stream data continuously. Services are for quick requests (like our Emergency Stop). Actions are for long-running tasks (e.g., "Navigate to coordinate X,Y", "Pick up a pallet", "Map this room").

docs.title: What is an Action?¶
docs.text:
The action are composed by three parts:

  1. Goal: The Client requests a task (e.g., "Drive to a goal position").
  2. Feedback: The Server sends continuous updates while working (e.g., "I have driven 0.5m... 1.0m...").
  3. Result: The Server sends a final confirmation when the task is done (e.g., "Success, arrived at destination"). Crucially, the Client can cancel the Goal at any time before the Result is reached.

docs.title: ROS2 Debug message with rtq_console¶
docs.text:
Every time you use self.get_logger().info(), warn(), or error(), ROS 2 does two things:

  1. It prints the string to your terminal.
  2. It publishes the string as a standardized message to a hidden topic called /rosout. When you have 20 nodes running across 3 different computers, looking at one terminal is useless and ineffective. We use a GUI tool called rqt_console to aggregate, filter, and search through every log message generated by every node in the network.

docs.title: RVIZ¶
docs.text: RVIZ is a data visualizer, it knows nothings about physics or robots but it can only drwas what ROS2 nodes is telling to the networks via topics or transforms. you already lunched a setupped version of RVIZ previously but if you want a clean window you need to type rviz2 in your terminal.

docs.title: Visualize the robot frames with TF¶
docs.text:
The most critical debugging tool in ROS 2 is the Transform Tree (TF2). A robot is made of a lots of links (base, wheels, laser, camera). TF2 keeps track of where every part is relative to every other part mathematically. If your laser scanner is working, but it thinks it is attached to the robot's rear instead of the front, your robot will drive backward into walls. Let's visualize the "Skeleton" of our amrj16 robot in RViz!

docs.title: ROS2 doctor¶
docs.text:
When things are getting bad, there is one tool that perform several checks to ensure everything is correct, this is called ros2 doctor. It analyzes your environment variables, network configuration, and package installations to find common errors. If ros2 doctor returns User WARNING or ERROR, it will usually tell you exactly what environment variable is missing or if multiple machines are colliding on the same DDS network domain.

docs.title: What you will learn
docs.text:
In this documentation, you will learn how to:

  • set up the development environment
  • install the required dependencies
  • configure the ROS 2 workspace
  • run basic examples
  • understand the J16MOTO software structure
  • work with topics, services, actions, and debugging tools
  • build up to the final autonomous docking application

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before starting, make sure you have:

  • a Linux machine or a supported development environment
  • ROS 2 installed
  • Python 3 available
  • Git installed
  • basic familiarity with terminal commands
  • access to the J16MOTO project files or repository

docs.title: Recommended environment
docs.text:
The tutorials are intended to work best with:

  • Ubuntu 22.04
  • ROS 2 Humble
  • Python 3.10+

Other setups may work, but the examples and commands in this documentation assume this baseline environment.

docs.title: Folder structure
docs.text:
A typical workspace may look like this:

```bash ~/j16moto_ws/ ├── src/ │ ├── j16moto_bringup/ │ ├── j16moto_description/ │ ├── j16moto_interfaces/ │ └── j16moto_examples/ ├── build/ ├── install/ └── log/

docs.title: ROS 2 Tutorials for MOTO Mobile Autonomous Robots
docs.text:
Welcome to the ROS 2 Tutorials section dedicated to the mobile autonomous robots developed by MOTO Mechatronics.

These tutorials are specifically designed for MOTO Mechatronics robotic platforms, with a particular focus on the J16MOTO robot.

The tutorials can be directly implemented using the MOTO Mechatronics Application Development Environment, available at: https://github.com/motomechatronics/amrj16moto_utimac

This section provides a structured and comprehensive learning path for students, researchers, and engineers aiming to acquire practical and professional competencies in mobile robotics using ROS 2, working on real robotic systems rather than abstract examples.

docs.title: Purpose of this Section
docs.text:
The objective of these tutorials is to:

  • Provide a structured and progressive introduction to ROS 2 applied to mobile robotics
  • Bridge the gap between theoretical knowledge and real robotic systems
  • Enable hands-on experimentation on MOTO Mechatronics platforms
  • Promote sound engineering practices, including reproducibility, clarity, and documentation

These tutorials are not intended as isolated exercises, but as part of a coherent learning pathway, where each topic builds upon the previous one.

docs.title: Target Platforms
docs.text:
The tutorials are developed and validated on mobile autonomous robots produced by MOTO Mechatronics, including educational and research-oriented platforms.

While the primary focus is on MOTO systems, the methodologies and workflows follow ROS 2 standards, making them transferable to other robotic platforms.

docs.title: Tutorial Scope
docs.text:
This section covers the core components of the ROS 2 ecosystem for mobile robotics:

  • ROS 2 Fundamentals
  • Nodes, topics, services, and actions
  • Parameters and lifecycle nodes

  • Package structure and best practices

  • Robot Description

  • URDF and Xacro
  • TF and coordinate frames

  • Modeling of sensors and actuators

  • Simulation

  • Gazebo and compatible simulators
  • Simulation versus real robot workflows

  • Debugging in simulated environments

  • Navigation

  • Nav2 stack
  • Localization (AMCL)
  • Mapping and SLAM

  • Costmaps and planning algorithms

  • Perception

  • Integration of cameras and LiDAR
  • Sensor data processing

  • Basic perception pipelines

  • System Integration

  • Launch files
  • Configuration management
  • Debugging and logging

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
Each tutorial is designed according to the following principles:

  • Applied – all commands and code are executed on real systems
  • Explanatory – each step is introduced with its underlying rationale
  • Incremental – complexity is introduced progressively
  • Reproducible – results should be consistent and verifiable

Users are encouraged to experiment actively, including modifying and troubleshooting the system as part of the learning process.

docs.title: Intended Audience
docs.text:
These tutorials are intended for:

  • University students in robotics, automation, or computer science
  • Internship candidates working with MOTO Mechatronics
  • Junior engineers approaching ROS 2 for mobile robotics
  • Practitioners seeking a clear and application-oriented introduction to ROS 2

A basic understanding of Linux and programming (Python and/or C++) is recommended.

docs.title: Learning Philosophy
docs.text:
ROS 2 is not only a middleware, but a framework for designing and reasoning about robotic systems.

These tutorials aim to develop both practical skills and the ability to analyze system architecture, data flow, and behavior in real-world conditions.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The tutorials are continuously updated and expanded
  • Feedback and contributions are encouraged
  • All content is intended for educational and training purposes

docs.title: Example: ROS 2 Node
docs.text: This section illustrates the creation and execution of a minimal ROS 2 node.

docs.title: Initialization
docs.text:
Note

Ensure that ROS 2 is correctly installed and that the environment has been sourced before executing any command.

docs.title: Running a Node
docs.text:
Tip

Execute the following command to run a ROS 2 node:

ros2 run my_package my_node

docs.title: Common Issue
docs.text:
Environment not sourced

If commands are not recognized, verify that the ROS 2 environment has been sourced:

source /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash

docs.title: Python Node Example
docs.text:
import rclpy
from rclpy.node import Node


class MyNode(Node):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__('my_node')
        self.get_logger().info("Hello ROS2")


def main(args=None):
    rclpy.init(args=args)
    node = MyNode()
    rclpy.spin(node)
    node.destroy_node()
    rclpy.shutdown()
